file_name: Shorfuzzaman - 2021 - An explainable stacked ensemble of deep learning models for improved melanoma skin cancer detection.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an explainable stacked ensemble of deep learning models (specifically CNNs like ResNet50, VGG-16, DenseNet121, Xception, and EfficientNetB0) for the classification of melanoma skin cancer from dermoscopic images. It also employs SHAP for explainability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel explainable stacked ensemble framework using CNNs (ResNet50, VGG-16, DenseNet121, Xception, EfficientNetB0) and SHAP for melanoma detection. It presents original experimental results, including training on the ISIC dataset (1497 malignant and 1800 benign images), and reports specific performance metrics (accuracy 95.76%, sensitivity 96.67%, AUC 0.957), indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: An explainable stacked ensemble of deep learning models for improved melanoma skin cancer detection
brief_summary: This study proposes an explainable stacked ensemble framework using deep convolutional neural networks to detect melanoma skin cancer from dermoscopic images. The model leverages transfer learning by assembling multiple CNN sub-models, specifically ResNet50, VGG-16, DenseNet121, Xception, and EfficientNetB0, with a meta-learner generating the final prediction. Training and validation were performed on a public Kaggle dataset derived from the ISIC archive, containing 1497 malignant and 1800 benign images. To address the black-box nature of deep learning, the authors employed SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) to produce heatmaps that visualize indicative areas for dermatologists. The proposed ensemble model achieved an accuracy of 95.76%, sensitivity of 96.67%, and an AUC of 0.957. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the explainable stacked ensemble approach for early melanoma detection compared to individual base models.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies malignant melanoma lesions. The dataset used contained 1497 images of malignant moles.
The study classifies benign moles as the negative class for melanoma detection. The dataset used contained 1800 images of benign moles.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma skin cancer (malignant vs. benign)
all_task_types.details: The paper proposes a stacked ensemble framework for the automated diagnosis of melanoma from dermoscopic images. The model performs a binary classification task to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign moles. The dataset consists of 1497 malignant and 1800 benign images. The primary output is a discrete diagnostic label (malignant or benign), supported by metrics such as accuracy (95.76%), sensitivity (96.67%), specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC (0.957). While the model outputs probabilities to determine the class, the task is classification, not regression.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle ISIC Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of skin lesions acquired through the International Skin Image Collection (ISIC) archive, hosted on Kaggle. It contains dermoscopic images of melanoma skin cancer lesions.

use.training: Used for training the stacking ensemble model (base CNNs and meta-learner).
use.validation: Used for validation of the stacking ensemble model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize skin lesion images in 2D to understand the separability of benign and malignant melanoma classes based on extracted features.
To evaluate the stability and discrimination ability of the models by plotting true positive rate (TPR) against false positive rate (FPR) at different threshold values.
To quantify True Positives (TP), False Positives (FP), True Negatives (TN), and False Negatives (FN) for evaluating classification performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC, along with confusion matrices and ROC curves.

Kaggle ISIC Skin Lesion Dataset.split: Validation Set (60% training, 20% validation, 20% testing split used for training/validation; results reported on validation set)
Kaggle ISIC Skin Lesion Dataset.experiment: Performance comparison of individual CNN sub-models vs. Stacked Ensemble Model
Kaggle ISIC Skin Lesion Dataset.notes: The stacked ensemble model outperformed individual CNN sub-models. The ensemble achieved an average AUC of 0.957. The study notes that the ensemble model produced fewer false positive and false negative cases compared to individual sub-models. SHAP values were used for explainability, visualizing indicative areas for malignant melanoma.

architecture_used.main_model: Explainable stacked ensemble of deep learning models (CNNs)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning concept used; base CNN models (EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, Xception) pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset

architecture_details.level_0: Three fine-tuned CNN sub-models (EfficientNetB0, DenseNet121, Xception) perform feature extraction and initial classification
architecture_details.level_1: Meta-learner combines prediction probability vectors from the first-level CNN sub-models to generate the final classification result
architecture_details.custom_layers: Classifier part of backbone CNNs replaced with a custom prediction layer consisting of a dropout layer and a dense layer with a single neuron

classification_or_output_head.final_layer: Dense layer with a single neuron (part of the custom prediction layer in base models) and meta-learner for final prediction

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: All images were rescaled to a uniform dimension of 224 × 224.
Normalization: Pixel intensity values were normalized to the range [0, 1].
Noise Removal: Gaussian blurring technique was applied using a Gaussian filter (low-pass filter) to reduce noise.
Contrast Enhancement: Contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE) was used to improve image contrast by stretching the pixels' distribution.
conclusions: The proposed explainable CNN-based stacked ensemble framework effectively detects melanoma skin cancer at earlier stages by combining multiple base CNN models (ResNet50, VGG-16, DenseNet121, Xception, and EfficientNetB0) with a meta-learner.
The stacked ensemble model outperformed individual CNN sub-models, achieving an accuracy of 95.76%, sensitivity of 96.67%, and an AUC of 0.957 on the validation set.
The ensemble model demonstrated better convergence of training and validation losses compared to individual CNN sub-models, indicating reduced overfitting despite the limited dataset size.

The integration of SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) provided interpretability by generating heatmaps that visualize the areas of melanoma images most indicative of the disease, addressing the 'black-box' nature of deep learning models for clinical acceptance.
The study utilized a publicly available Kaggle dataset derived from the ISIC archive, containing 1497 malignant and 1800 benign mole images, for training and validation.
Data augmentation techniques (random rotation, random flip, re-scaling) and dropout regularization were effective in preventing overfitting in the stacked ensemble model.

The approach offers a robust and intelligent method for automated diagnosis, potentially supporting both novice and expert dermatologists by reducing the probability of erroneous diagnoses associated with manual ABCD rule application.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single, publicly available Kaggle dataset derived from the ISIC archive (1497 malignant, 1800 benign images) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting generalizability.
The dataset size is small (3,297 total images), which increases the risk of overfitting despite the use of data augmentation and dropout regularization.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals to support the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.
The model was trained and validated on a single split (60% training, 20% validation, 20% testing) without mention of k-fold cross-validation, potentially introducing split-specific bias.

The dataset contains only binary classes (benign vs. malignant), excluding other skin lesions (e.g., seborrheic keratosis, basal cell carcinoma) that are common differential diagnoses in clinical practice, limiting real-world applicability.
The explainability method (SHAP) provides heatmaps for interpretability, but the clinical utility of these visualizations for dermatologists is not empirically validated in the study.

The study claims to use five CNNs (ResNet50, VGG-16, DenseNet121, Xception, EfficientNetB0) in the introduction but only stacks three (DenseNet121, Xception, EfficientNetB0) in the final ensemble architecture, creating a discrepancy in reported methodology.
No information is provided regarding potential data leakage between training and validation/test sets, which is critical given the small dataset size and potential overlap in public ISIC subsets.
